figures.caption: Fig. 1. Architecture of uncertainty propagation and risk measure computation for Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI). A Gaussian distribution is propagated through time using both parametric and neural network components. The cost of a trajectory is calculated using a risk measure computed on the empirical distribution of costs generated from sampling using the unscented transform.

Fig. 2. State trajectory and sigma point plots of the HW model with and without the closed-loop gains. In Fig. 2a True state values are in orange, the predicted trajectory by the model is in blue, the green circles are the open-loop xy uncertainty ellipses, and the purple indicates the closed-loop uncertainty. Figs. 2c and 2d show plots of the states and the sigma points are shown in their respective colors along with a red line indicating the mean error of the model.

Fig. 4. Box plots showing total summed NLL loss for various structural changes to the networks. Whiskers are defined as ±1.5IQR and is given with the arrows, the green line defines the median and the orange the mean value. The baseline model (bline) is kept consistent. The explanations of the keys used in shown in the text Section V-A1.

Fig. 13. Box plots showing vy error at the end of the 5s trajectory for various structural changes to the networks. Whiskers are defined as ±1.5IQR and is given with the arrows, the green line defines the median and the orange the mean value. The baseline model (bline) is kept consistent. The explanations of the keys used in shown in the text Section V-A1.
Fig. 33. Full set of images from marker 2 in Fig. 9.

Fig. 14. Box plots showing ψ̇ error at the end of the 5s trajectory for various structural changes to the networks. Whiskers are defined as ±1.5IQR and is given with the arrows, the green line defines the median and the orange the mean value. The baseline model (bline) is kept consistent. The explanations of the keys used in shown in the text Section V-A1.

Fig. 6. A view into the long traverse discused in Section V-B1. The left shows the top down speed map of vx for the entire traverse while the left shows images pulled from the camera during the run. Images are marked in purple with the number that corresponds to the marker in the top down image where they were pulled from.
Fig. 18. Full set of images from marker 2 in Fig. 6.
Fig. 17. Full set of images from marker 1 in Fig. 6.

Whiskers are defined as ±1.5IQR and is given with the arrows, the green line defines the median and the orange the mean value. The baseline model (bline) is kept consistent. Fig. 25. Full set of images from marker 3 in Fig. 7.
Fig. 26. Full set of images from marker 4 in Fig. 7.

Fig. 5. Box plots showing total summed NLL loss for various changes to the size or inputs of the network. Whiskers are defined as ±1.5IQR and is given with the arrows, the green lines defines the median and the orange the mean value. The baseline model (bline) is kept consistent between all graphs. Predictor Hidden Size Fig. 5a shows the impact of varying the hidden size of the predictor network. Predictor output width Fig. 5b and depth Fig. 5c show the impact of changing the output network of the predictor. The values are either the size of the output network layer or their multiple layers when separated by an . The final comparison Fig. 5d shows the model ran on hardware (HW), the baseline model, and an improved baseline models from Section V-A3.
Fig. 24. Full set of images from marker 2 in Fig. 7.
Fig. 23. Full set of images from marker 1 in Fig. 7.
Fig. 28. Full set of images from marker 2 in Fig. 8.
Fig. 27. Full set of images from marker 1 in Fig. 8.

Fig. 9. Shows top down view of vx and images for the different runs of the trail course outlined in Section V-B3. The top down map and images are marked by numbers in purple that correlate with the map shown to the left of it. Fig. 9a and Fig. 9b is the first and Fig. 9d and Fig. 9e is the second pair taken from the same location and can be compared between T1 and T2. T1 is using cσ = 2.0 and T2 uses cσ = 1.0 but otherwise they are identical stacks run around the same time of day.

Fig. 11. Box plots showing ψ error at the end of the 5s trajectory for various structural changes to the networks. Whiskers are defined as ±1.5IQR and is given with the arrows, the green line defines the median and the orange the mean value. The baseline model (bline) is kept consistent. The explanations of the keys used in shown in the text Section V-A1.

Fig. 3. A comparison of open loop trajectories and sigma points from the three different models used in Fig. 5d and explained in Section V-A3. The top image shows the different models mean and uncertainty ellipse in their respective colors along with the ground truth in orange. The bottom row shows the graphical representation of the sigma points.

Fig. 7. Speed maps for the first case study discussed in Section V-B2. Fig. 7a shows the vx values while Fig. 7b shows the vy values. Relevant trees are marked in the images and in the top down map to indicate where events occur. Images are marked in purple with a number that corresponds to the top down image.
